data.title: Shortest Mode
data.statement:
Congratulations, you've just coded a solution to a clash in <<CGL>> (a made-up coding language)! Unfortunately, you're playing the clash in Shortest Mode, which requires solutions to contain as few characters as possible.

To create the shortest possible code, you come up with a three-step plan:

    1. <<Remove all whitespace characters>> from your code, except for newline (line break) characters, which you must <<replace with semi-colons>> ({{\n}} → {{;}}) for syntax correctness.

    2. <<Reduce variable names to a single character>>. Variables in CGL consist only of English letters ({{A}}-{{Z}}, {{a}}-{{z}}) and do not contain other non-alphabetical characters (e.g., digits or underscores). You intend to <<use the first letter (with casing preserved)>> as the shortened variable name.

    3. Numbers <<ending with some consecutive number of zeros>> can be converted into standard notation <<if the resulting notation is shorter>>. The standard notation of a number (e.g., {{1750000}}) is the non-zero starting portion of the number, followed by {{e}}, followed by the number of ending consecutive zeros (e.g., {{175e4}}).

Hopefully your code will now be short enough to win! Good luck!

testCases.title: Validator 2
testCases.testIn:
6
Loop = 5
Loop * {
someVar = Loop + 1
::<< someVar
Loop += Loop + someVar
}

testCases.title: Spacey Operators
testCases.testIn:
4
sPaCeY = $(  :@  notAnotherOne  )
operate = " ${  sPaCeY } && aGAIN"
::<<   operate   <<    notAnotherOne
Det += operate

testCases.title: Validator 3
testCases.testIn:
2
ReP   =   INF  *   neg   -     1
::<<  ReP <<  ReP   <<    ReP <<ReP

testCases.title: Financial Matters
testCases.testIn:
6
HOUSE = $3000000
CAR = $50007
swimmingPool = $8650
necklace = $107000
total = HOUSE+CAR+swimmingPool+necklace
::<<total

testCases.title: Validator 4
testCases.testIn:
7
BudGet     = $450000
gOlf       = $76 * 3000
spare      = Budget - gOlf - tax * 70%
PERCENTAGE = 6/5 * 15000 - 50% * 60 ^ 1720004
spare     /= PERCENTAGE
::<< BudGet << gOlf << PERCENTAGE
::<< spare

testCases.title: Validator 5

data.constraints:
• 1 ≤ [[numLines]] ≤ 10
• 1 ≤ Input line length ≤ 50
• 1 ≤ Output length ≤ 100
• Whitespace characters used in the input will be limited to spaces, newlines ({{\n}}), and tabs ({{\t}}).
• Every occurrence of an English letter ({{A}}-{{Z}}, {{a}}-{{z}}) will always be part of some variable.
• The standard notation of a number will never have the same length as the original number.
• All numbers are valid 32-bit integers.
data.stubGenerator:
read numLines:int
loop numLines read codeRow:string(50)
write shortest CGL code
data.inputDescription:
<<Line 1>>: A positive integer [[numLines]]; the number of lines of CGL code.
<<Next [[numLines]] lines>>: The original CGL code.
data.outputDescription: <<Line 1>>: The shortened CGL code, after applying the three-step plan as stated above.

lastVersion.statementHTML:
Goal

Congratulations, you've just coded a solution to a clash in CGL (a made-up coding language)! Unfortunately, you're playing the clash in Shortest Mode, which requires solutions to contain as few characters as possible.

To create the shortest possible code, you come up with a three-step plan:

1. Remove all whitespace characters from your code, except for newline (line break) characters, which you must replace with semi-colons (\n → ;) for syntax correctness.

2. Reduce variable names to a single character. Variables in CGL consist only of English letters (A-Z, a-z) and do not contain other non-alphabetical characters (e.g., digits or underscores). You intend to use the first letter (with casing preserved) as the shortened variable name.

3. Numbers ending with some consecutive number of zeros can be converted into standard notation if the resulting notation is shorter. The standard notation of a number (e.g., 1750000) is the non-zero starting portion of the number, followed by e, followed by the number of ending consecutive zeros (e.g., 175e4).

Hopefully your code will now be short enough to win! Good luck!
Input
Line 1: A positive integer numLines; the number of lines of CGL code.
Next numLines lines: The original CGL code.
Output
Line 1: The shortened CGL code, after applying the three-step plan as stated above.
Constraints
• 1 ≤ numLines ≤ 10
• 1 ≤ Input line length ≤ 50
• 1 ≤ Output length ≤ 100
• Whitespace characters used in the input will be limited to spaces, newlines (\n), and tabs (\t).
• Every occurrence of an English letter (A-Z, a-z) will always be part of some variable.
• The standard notation of a number will never have the same length as the original number.
• All numbers are valid 32-bit integers.
Example
Input
3
first = 1
second = 2
(first == second) ? first : second
Output
f=1;s=2;(f==s)?f:s
